video_title: Contrarian Quality at GQG Partners – Rajiv Jain
gist: GQG Partners CIO Rajiv Jain argues the AI/hyperscaler trade has dangerous economics — ~$1T/year capex against ~$70-80B AI revenue, collapsing free cash flow, and no pricing power — and has exited semis and most Big Tech in favor of contrarian 'forward-looking quality': energy, regulated utilities, European steel, tobacco, enterprise software at ~10x earnings, and emerging markets. He frames quality as barriers to entry, runs an absolute-return mindset with journalist-analysts compensated to disagree, and sizes positions like a credit analyst to avoid blowups.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers and AI labs are spending roughly $1 trillion per year in capex against only ~$70-80 billion of AI revenue, a ratio Jain argues makes the AI buildout economically unsustainable.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Frames the AI capex cycle as a value trap; watch for the revenue/capex gap to force a repricing of hyperscaler and AI-infrastructure equities.
insights.quote: You're spending a trillion dollars a year and the revenue on AI talk about maybe 70 80 billion.

insights.claim: The cumulative lifetime capex of the mega-cap tech companies is about $1.5 trillion, yet they now propose spending ~$3 trillion in just three years, inverting the historically capital-light nature of these businesses.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Capital intensity is structurally rising for businesses long valued as asset-light compounders, which should compress their multiples and returns on capital.
insights.quote: The cumulative capex of all these mag companies in their history is $1 half trillion. Think about it. Now they're talking about three trillion in just three years.

insights.claim: Google's free cash flow has collapsed to roughly $10 billion (excluding buybacks) because AI capex is now running faster than cloud revenue growth, and the prior $50-60 billion of buybacks is gone.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Erosion of Google's free cash flow undercuts the buyback support for the stock and signals deteriorating business quality across hyperscalers.
insights.quote: Google Ascore had $10 billion free cash flow clean that's it that's without the share buyback by the way they used to buy 50 $60 billion that's out of the window

insights.claim: Nvidia's headline free cash flow is overstated because it reinvested ~$25 billion into startups and its own customers (over 50 in the last 6-9 months), effectively making those investments a hidden form of capex needed to sustain demand.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Circular vendor-financing means Nvidia's real free cash flow is lower than reported; watch customer-investment disclosures as a tell on demand durability.
insights.quote: Nvidia invested over 50 plus of their customers in last 69 months. Nothing wrong with that, but the free cash is a lot lower.

insights.claim: Half or more of hyperscaler cloud backlog and revenue is coming from OpenAI and Anthropic, and Jain believes much of it is circular (e.g. Amazon invests in Anthropic via compute credits later spent at Amazon).
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: If AI-lab demand is circular and concentrated, cloud backlog quality is far worse than reported and could reverse sharply if labs cut spend.
insights.quote: Half or more of revenue is coming from OpenAI/anthropic.

insights.claim: OpenAI has effectively committed ~$1 trillion against perhaps $20 billion of revenue, and Anthropic and SpaceX-type private AI plays sit in 'a whole different league' of valuation versus their cash generation.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Private AI valuations are detached from revenue; an investor should treat them as binary bets, not compounders, and watch for markdowns.
insights.quote: How in the world open have invested trillion dollars when your revenue is maybe 20 billion?

insights.claim: X.AI had cash losses of ~$12-15 billion with only 11% capacity utilization on its Colossus cluster and is now reselling capacity to Anthropic, evidence the buildout has overshot real demand.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Low utilization plus capacity resale signals oversupply in AI compute, undermining the 'compute shortage' narrative.
insights.quote: X.AI cash losses were give and take double digit billions 12 to 15 and their capacity relation on the colossus was 11%. Now they're selling the capacity to entropic.

insights.claim: AI compute has no pricing power: GPU rental prices barely cover GPU costs, and the apparent compute shortage is an artifact of subsidized underpricing rather than genuine scarcity.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Returns on AI capex must be lower than consensus; the true scarcity test only comes once tokens are priced to cover cost.
insights.quote: When you're underpricing everything, if you look at Corv and Nebas, they're bleeding heavily. That means they're not covering the cost. The real test of shortage is when you price appropriately.

insights.claim: Google's reported operating margin would be high-single-digits rather than 23% if it had not changed its depreciation policy three years ago, masking the true profitability hit from AI infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Accounting depreciation extensions are flattering hyperscaler margins; scrutinize useful-life assumptions when modeling AI-era earnings.
insights.quote: If you look at Google, if they had not changed the depreciation policy, the margin would be high single digits operating margin.

insights.claim: Digital advertising is near saturation, with major consumer-staples advertisers saying their digital spend is ~95-100% penetrated and shifting toward point-of-sale (Walmart-type) channels, capping the ad-driven model behind Google and Meta.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Ad-revenue growth runway is closing for the platforms even before AI costs, a headwind to the bull case for ad-funded Big Tech.
insights.quote: our digital advertising is almost 95 100% penetrated we're not going to increase in fact we going to shift to point of sales more like Walmart type stuff

insights.claim: GQG's former-journalist analysts are even more bearish on AI than its traditional analysts, drawing explicit parallels to the leverage dynamics of the mortgage crisis.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: An internal red-team comparing AI to 2008 leverage suggests systemic, not just valuation, risk in the trade.
insights.quote: the journalists are extremely bearish on the AI stuff. Their view is there a lot of similarities with the mortgage crisis in terms of the amount of leverage involved

insights.claim: Enterprises cannot fully adopt AI for core workflows because LLMs are probabilistic and still hallucinate at meaningful rates; Goldman ran parallel systems for years and accuracy remains ~80-85%, insufficient where real numbers and licenses are at stake.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Hallucination caps near-term enterprise AI ROI, supporting the durability of incumbent enterprise software rather than its disruption.
insights.quote: In an enterprise world, you can't use a probabilistic system.

insights.claim: Jain defines quality strictly as barriers to entry that protect return on capital over a full cycle, and explicitly accepts cyclicality (e.g. European steel) as compatible with quality.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Reframes 'quality growth' away from secular tech toward moated cyclicals, explaining GQG's unusual portfolio.
insights.quote: Quality is barriers to entry.

insights.claim: Quality must be forward-looking, not backward-looking: energy and steel have become higher-barrier businesses (permitting, pipelines) while software and especially semiconductors have become lower-barrier than investors assume.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Direction of barrier-to-entry change, not historical reputation, is the screening signal — favoring out-of-favor cyclicals over crowded tech.
insights.quote: it should be forward-looking quality not backward-looking quality

insights.claim: Semiconductors are a lower-barrier-to-entry business than perceived because the Chinese are aggressively ramping capacity in semicap equipment and memory, and the lesson is that Chinese competition leads to overproduction that kills returns.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Use Chinese entry as a sell signal in any sector; specifically a reason GQG holds almost no semis despite past large exposure.
insights.quote: One of the big lessons investing is if Chinese are a competitor, be very careful because they will overproduce and kill you.

insights.claim: Software is generally a low-barrier business where very few companies survive 30+ years (Microsoft and maybe Oracle being exceptions), so durability must be proven rather than assumed.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Treat software longevity skeptically; only pay up where switching costs and regulatory lock-in are demonstrable.
insights.quote: How many companies have survived in software business over 30 plus years? Microsoft is an exception and maybe Oracle it's a low barrier to business.

insights.claim: Management quality is hard to assess, so Jain now meets management less, lets the financial record do the talking, and deliberately keeps some analysts away from management meetings to preserve critical distance.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: A process safeguard against being charmed by good salespeople — numbers remain the defining factor over narrative.
insights.quote: If I'm not met them, chance I'll be more critical.

insights.claim: GQG published a 2022 paper titled 'Is software the new shale?' and has now deployed almost $10 billion into enterprise software in the most recent month, betting against the thesis that AI will disrupt incumbents.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Signals a major, recent capital rotation into beaten-down enterprise software as a contrarian quality bet.
insights.quote: In last month, we put almost 10 billion dollars to work in software.

themes.theme: Contrarian positions: energy, utilities, tobacco, software

insights.claim: Jain is bullish on energy equities trading at double-digit free cash flow yields (15-20%) at $75-80 oil, arguing physical oil at $110-120 and realizations $10-20 above futures mean prices won't durably revert to $70.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Energy offers high cash yields with management refusing to add capacity; a backwardation/realization gap to watch as a catalyst.
insights.quote: we have almost nothing in semiconductors. We have almost nothing in tech.

insights.claim: Qatar has stated ~20% of its LNG facilities are down and will take 3-5 years to fix, supporting structurally tighter energy supply independent of any resolution of the Hormuz/war situation.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: A multi-year supply constraint underpins the energy thesis even if geopolitical risk premium fades.
insights.quote: 20% of thereabouts of their LG facilities are down, they'll take 3 to 5 years to fix them

insights.claim: Jain favors regulated utilities globally (Brazil, US, Europe, Asia ex-China) because the world has woefully underinvested in power infrastructure, offering 8-10% EPS growth visibility over 5-10 years — faster than the S&P's ~8% last decade.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Regulated utilities at 17-18x with 3-3.5% yields can compound double digits — a lower-risk growth alternative to AI plays.
insights.quote: You can buy utilities in US with giving you 5 to 10 year visibility of 8 to 10% EPS growth. That's faster than S&P.

insights.claim: Jain warns the recent memory-price spike is driving S&P earnings upgrades that won't last, because memory is highly cyclical and Chinese capacity additions are setting up price-demand destruction.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Index earnings upgrades tied to memory are a head-fake; fade the cyclical earnings boost rather than extrapolate it.
insights.quote: I'm probably excluding the recent spike in memory prices leading to S&P earnings upgrades. That won't last. Memories as cyclical as they come.

insights.claim: GQG has invested heavily in tobacco; Altria has outperformed Meta and now Microsoft and Amazon over the last five years despite US cigarette volumes declining 7-8% annually, because cash generation matters more than volume.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Declining-volume, high-cash-return businesses can beat secular growers — a concrete contrarian compounding example.
insights.quote: Altria has outperformed meta I think now Microsoft and Amazon last 5 years the cash generation matters

insights.claim: GQG owns no large US money-center banks because they trade at some of the highest price-to-book and price-to-revenue valuations in 25 years.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: A clear avoid-list: large-cap US financials are richly valued on historical metrics despite being a consensus holding.
insights.quote: we don't own any US banks large Wall Street money center banks they're all trading at some of the highest valuations on a price to book or price to revenue in 25 years

insights.claim: Adobe, which GQG flagged as overvalued at ~50x earnings in late 2021, now trades near 10x clean adjusted earnings while still growing double digits — illustrating how disruption fears have overshot fundamentals in enterprise software.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Adobe is presented as a model of the software re-rating opportunity: durable growth at a fraction of prior multiples.
insights.quote: here we are. It's on a clean adjusted basis 10 times earnings and the business growing double digits

insights.claim: Regulatory, compliance and licensing requirements (e.g. Autodesk for licensed architects) make incumbent enterprise software impossible to replace with 'vibe-coded' alternatives, protecting the installed base.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Regulatory lock-in is the moat behind the software thesis; favor mission-critical, license-bound vendors.
insights.quote: If somebody can lose their license if there's a problem, for example, if you're an architect, you have no choice. You have to use autoes products

insights.claim: The emerging-market index has become 'a leveraged version of semiconductor,' with four names now making up nearly a third of the index and concentrated memory/chip exposure US investors would never tolerate domestically.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Passive EM exposure is a concentrated tech bet; active stock-picking outside the index is where the alpha sits.
insights.quote: If you look at emerging market index that's like a leveraged version of semiconductor.

insights.claim: Jain is very bullish on Brazil, citing a ~$100 billion family-owned bank at 7-8x earnings with a 7% dividend yield that has earned 15%+ real return on equity for 30 years.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Highlights deep value plus durable returns in Brazilian financials versus richly-valued US banks.
insights.quote: that's the only bank I know which has not earned 15% real return equity for 30 years I don't know of any other bank family owned

insights.claim: GQG owned Petrobras heavily over the last five years, buying at a ~35% dividend yield; it now trades around a 12% dividend yield at six times earnings on $75 oil.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Concrete example of EM energy delivering extreme cash yields with irreplaceable assets; still cheap at 6x earnings.
insights.quote: Petro which we owned in a big way in last 5 years when we bought it was 35% dividend deal it today 12% dividend deal $75 oil it's six times earnings

insights.claim: Jain avoids Samsung as a 'classic' value trap because the semiconductor industry never rings a bell at tops and China is adding memory capacity aggressively, noting GQG had sold out of memory years before the recent rally.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Even cheap-looking memory names are avoided on supply-cycle grounds; sentiment swung from 'massive blood' to euphoria in six months.
insights.quote: Why would you own Samsung which is as classic as it gets now everybody's ramping up capacity? They never ring a bell in semiconductor industry

insights.claim: Several large emerging economies have outgrown developed peers — Brazil's economy is larger than Italy's, India is a ~$4.5 trillion GDP system, and some Indonesian banks are now larger than European banks — yet remain under-followed.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: The economic center of gravity is shifting from G7 to a broader G13/G20, where neglect creates absolute-return opportunities.
insights.quote: if you look at Brazil is larger than Italy

themes.theme: Process: team, journalists, and risk control

insights.claim: GQG employs an equal number of former investigative journalists as traditional analysts, compensating them so they cannot agree with the firm's theses by default — a structural red team born from the 2008 lesson that the press flagged the mortgage crisis early.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Institutionalized dissent via incentive design is a replicable model for combating groupthink in investment teams.
insights.quote: You want to structure the compensation where actually by default they cannot agree with you.

insights.claim: Jain deliberately hired analysts from the long/short hedge fund world with 10-15 years of experience precisely because they were unlikely to agree with him, unlike the home-grown team he trained at Vontobel.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Hiring for disagreement and outside experience is a core design choice differentiating GQG's culture.
insights.quote: we hired a lot of folks from the long short world particularly folks who had experienced 105 years. Why? Because I thought what is the chance they will agree with me? None.

insights.claim: GQG sizes positions like a credit analyst assigning ratings: a monoline or narrow business (e.g. Occidental) can never carry a top-weight position the way a diversified AAA-type business (e.g. Exxon) can, so sizing reflects business stability rather than pure conviction.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: A disciplined position-sizing rule that prevents blowups by capping concentration in narrow, fragile businesses.
insights.quote: the sizing should be based on how a credit analyst would think.

insights.claim: Jain runs a long-only book with an absolute-return mindset, arguing that over the long run nobody pays bills with relative performance and the unacceptable outcome is 'market down 40 and we down 43.'
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Explains GQG's willingness to deviate hugely from benchmarks and cut losses — survival depends on absolute, not relative, returns.
insights.quote: You don't pay bills with relative performance.

insights.claim: GQG deliberately lowered its hit rate versus Jain's Vontobel era because a high hit rate implies too high a bar that causes the team to miss multibaggers, so taking more chances with small positions is intentional.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive: optimizing for fewer high-conviction wins forgoes upside; tolerating more misses captures asymmetric payoffs.
insights.quote: if you lower the hit rate, that means you're also taking more chances

insights.claim: GQG operates only in large, liquid names because the team makes many mistakes and needs the ability to exit and re-enter; portfolios run ~30-35 names in the US book with the top 10 making up about half.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Liquidity-first construction enables fast mind-changing and risk reduction at scale ($160B AUM).
insights.quote: you only operate large liquid names. We make too many mistakes. Allows us to change our mind.

insights.claim: Jain rejects specialists and large teams, arguing specialists are usually wrong at major inflection points and that alpha comes from small teams of generalists who can compare across sectors.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Contrarian org-design view: small generalist teams over big specialist ones — a differentiator at $160B scale.
insights.quote: specialists at major inflection points are usually wrong

insights.claim: Decision-making uses four portfolio managers with equal votes and no consensus requirement; Jain holds a rarely-used veto, and every PM's stock-picking track record by area is tracked for transparency.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Distributed, tracked decision rights reduce key-person risk and surface who has edge in which markets.
insights.quote: we have a team of four portfolio managers. I have equal vote. I don't have an extreme super. I have a veto, but I'd really use that veto.

themes.theme: Adapting, cutting losses, and changing your mind

insights.claim: Jain's biggest losses historically came from cyclical businesses bought at high multiples on peak margins — e.g. Japanese names bought at 50x earnings on 25% margins that reverted to 5% margins and 8-9x multiples.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: A direct warning against today's cyclicals at high multiples on peak margins — the same setup that caused his worst losses.
insights.quote: the names that were cyclical and we paid high multiples on peak margins.

insights.claim: GQG sold semiconductors at the lows in October 2022 (a 10-15% book position, booking a loss) amid US-China restrictions, then re-entered in March-April 2023 as ChatGPT and improving data changed the picture — illustrating willingness to flip after admitting a mistake.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a low-ego, fast-reversal process; owning a name (even at a loss) sharpens conviction for re-entry.
insights.quote: We must have sold literally at the lows. So we booked a loss and sold out.

insights.claim: GQG started buying airlines (notably Delta) in December 2019 and oil stocks at low cash-cost breakevens, then cut both quickly in early 2020 as COVID spread — booking losses just before oil went negative, which freed capital to re-enter in late 2020/early 2021.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Fast loss-cutting preserved capital and pre-positioned the firm to rotate into the post-COVID energy recovery.
insights.quote: We cut our losses quickly on the airlines Delta specifically

insights.claim: Jain exited financials/banks (Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, AIG, Anglo Irish Bank) in early 2007 but held too much energy on a decoupling thesis, which then collapsed in October 2008 — teaching him to connect cross-sector second-order effects.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Recognizing one risk isn't enough; the lesson is to trace contagion across sectors — relevant to today's concentrated AI exposure.
insights.quote: I recognized the financial issues but didn't connect the dots on the energy side

insights.claim: GQG exited technology entirely in 2021 when it judged semiconductors over-earning, then re-entered AI names in 2023 after ChatGPT — and now sits at near-zero tech and semis again, showing it has no permanent attachment to any sector.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Sector exposure swings to near-zero or near-40% by design; the willingness to move is itself the edge.
insights.quote: We exited 2021 extremely underway technology.

insights.claim: Jain's single biggest evolution over his career is greater humility — conviction levels have gone down and he now relies far more on others poking holes and debating, treating internal dissent as core risk management.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: The CIO's stated worldview: lower conviction plus structured disagreement is the survival formula at scale.
insights.quote: you begin to appreciate how little you know. You become humbler because the conviction level actually goes down.

insights.claim: Jain's biggest investment pet peeve is dogmatism and deep ideologies ('growth will always work,' 'cheap will always work'), which he calls the most dangerous trait; GQG tries not to hire dogmatic people and rewards changing one's mind.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Be dogmatic about the math, not about views — a hiring and culture filter that explains GQG's flexibility.
insights.quote: be dogmatic about the math rather than dogmatic about our views

themes.theme: Valuation framework and market concentration risk

insights.claim: GQG's investment hurdle is a high-single to low-double-digit expected return (rule of thumb 9-11%) derived from current free cash flow yield assuming no multiple expansion, which is how it justified avoiding Adobe at 50x and buying it at 10x.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: A concrete underwriting rule: if the cash yield alone doesn't compound to ~10%, multiple expansion is required and the bet is fragile.
insights.quote: how do we get double digit expected return high single digit double digit 9 to 11% is my rule of thumb

insights.claim: Exxon and AMD have similar market caps, yet Exxon may generate ~$50 billion of clean free cash flow at $75 oil versus AMD's roughly $9 billion, a valuation gap Jain uses to argue the math favors energy over AI hardware.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: A direct relative-value trade idea: long the cash machine (Exxon) over the hyped chip name (AMD) on free-cash-flow grounds.
insights.quote: Exxon is almost similar market cap as AMD depending on the oil prices this year they generate probably 50 billion of clean free cash flow AMD will be lucky if they generate nine

insights.claim: Jain sees today's setup echoing 2010, when starting from ~25-30x earnings investors lost two-thirds of their money in Microsoft over the prior decade, implying poor long-term returns from current elevated valuations.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: High starting valuations historically produced lost decades even in great companies; a caution against buying the index at today's multiples.
insights.quote: in 2010 you start with 10 times earnings from 25 times earnings maybe 30 depending on what values you look at 2000 2010 you lost 2/3 of Microsoft

insights.claim: Crowding spans public equities, private equity, and private credit, with the private world arguably worse — Jain cites Figma falling 80-90% after listing and SpaceX at a ~$1 trillion-area valuation on $18 billion revenue and a $5 billion loss.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: The concentration risk is systemic across asset classes; private marks may understate the danger seen when assets list publicly.
insights.quote: private world is worse in a way

insights.claim: Jain warns it is better to leave a hyped trade early because, given extreme concentration across public and private markets, you won't be able to exit in size once a catalyst hits.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Liquidity/exit risk justifies pre-emptive de-risking from crowded AI exposure even at the cost of near-term underperformance.
insights.quote: you're better off leaving early if you're running as sis because it's hard to exit on the other side

themes.theme: Firm building, alignment, and going public

insights.claim: GQG keeps fees below median by design — likened to a 'Costco model' — because efficient markets leave little excess return and high fees structurally erode the net performance that determines survival.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Low, hard-to-raise fees are a deliberate competitive moat for an active manager in a fee-compressed industry.
insights.quote: It's kind of like a Costco model manage with lower income rather than having the income that you can't cut it afterwards.

insights.claim: GQG prohibits single-stock personal trading and requires significant employee skin in the game, with Jain holding almost all of his net worth in the same GQG products clients buy.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Total alignment via co-investment and no personal trading is a structural trust signal for allocators.
insights.quote: we don't allow any personal trading here. Everybody has significant skin in the game.

insights.claim: GQG grew from launch in 2016 to ~$160 billion AUM in about a decade, an outcome Jain calls a complete surprise, driven by performance, heavy distribution investment under CEO Tim Carver, intense client servicing, and a differentiated absolute-return ethos.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Demonstrates active management can still scale fast through distribution and crisis-driven differentiation, not just track record.
insights.quote: It's been a complete surprise.

insights.claim: GQG listed in Australia in 2021 primarily because of its early Australian client base and because Australia requires only semi-annual reporting, which Jain prefers since quarterly reporting multiplies work without adding much value.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Going public gave a compensation/transition currency while keeping ~75% insider ownership to avoid being hostage to Wall Street sentiment.
insights.quote: they only report twice a year. I said that'll be a lot less work.

insights.claim: In his first letter as the company's largest shareholder, Jain committed that GQG would never have AUM targets or margin targets, to avoid the asset-gathering behaviors that reduce alpha opportunity.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Explicitly subordinating asset growth to performance is a governance commitment allocators can hold the firm to.
insights.quote: We will never have AUM targets and we never have margin targets

insights.claim: GQG has had some redemptions in the last six months after underperforming across all books, but still took in net new money for the ninth straight year and continued to compound in the mid-teens without losing money.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Underperformance without absolute losses is tolerated by clients; the real risk is losing money in down markets, not lagging the benchmark.
insights.quote: In last six odd months, we've had some redemptions. We still had net new money last year for ninth year.

insights.claim: Jain keeps equity ownership concentrated at senior levels and pays younger employees mostly in cash so the firm's people don't fixate on the share price, preserving investment focus.
insights.speaker: Rajiv Jain
insights.so_what: Deliberate compensation design insulates the investment process from public-market stock-price swings.
insights.quote: you don't want everybody looking at stock price. Cash, which is cash.
